ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for red ruby dessert (thap thim krop). Mapped to broader 'dessert food product' class. A more specific term covering tapioca-coated water chestnut desserts in coconut milk is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKK (Sweet dishes and desserts) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for tapioca-coated water chestnut dessert in coconut milk within FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific dessert preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of red ruby dessert is approximately 250 g, comprising chilled coconut milk syrup with tapioca-coated water chestnut pieces served over shaved ice.

nutrient_source.title: Red Ruby Dessert — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Red Ruby Dessert (cr_id F02050018). Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, and calcium are taken as reported. Iron was reported as -1 (not available) in the source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for red ruby dessert as a composite dish. GI estimated at 75 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor: tapioca starch (tapioca flour coating). Tapioca starch GI is reported at approximately 70–95 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A midpoint estimate of 75 is used, reflecting dilution by water chestnut (lower GI ~60) and coconut milk (negligible carbohydrate contribution). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 14.6) / 100 = 10.9, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 5.83 × 2.5 = 14.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.9 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium is low at 18 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g due to the high saturated fat content from coconut milk, which can contribute to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure over time, even though sodium content is low.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI tapioca starch coating (estimated GI ~75) combined with added sugar syrup causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit red ruby dessert, as the tapioca flour coating has a high glycaemic index (~75) and the added sugar syrup further accelerates glucose absorption, making blood glucose management difficult even at moderate serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.9 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol risk; total fat is 5.58 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume red ruby dessert only occasionally and in small portions, as the saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.9 g/100 g) can raise LDL cholesterol levels and increase cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.9 g/100 g) may promote pro-inflammatory cytokines; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of red ruby dessert, as the high saturated fat content from coconut milk may promote inflammatory pathways, and the high-GI tapioca starch contributes to systemic glycaemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red ruby dessert (Thai: ทับทิมกรอบ, Thap Thim Krop) is a popular Southeast Asian chilled dessert widely served in Singapore and Thailand. It consists of diced water chestnuts coated in tapioca flour and red food colouring, which are briefly boiled until the tapioca coating becomes translucent and jewel-like, resembling rubies. The coated water chestnut pieces are served over shaved ice and submerged in sweetened coconut milk syrup. The dessert has a distinctive dual texture: the tapioca coating is soft and slightly chewy, while the water chestnut interior remains crisp and refreshing. The flavour is mildly sweet with a rich, creamy coconut milk base. Per 100 g, the dish provides 76 kcal, 5.58 g fat (predominantly saturated fat ~4.9 g from coconut milk), 5.83 g carbohydrate, 4.27 g sugar, 0.63 g protein, 1.59 g dietary fibre, and 18 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 75 (high) due to the tapioca starch coating. Clinically, this dessert is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes due to the high-GI tapioca starch and added sugar syrup. The saturated fat content from coconut milk raises moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. It is gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (one bowl).

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The source match is exact for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on tapioca starch GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component to medium; however, the nutrient data confidence remains high overall.
notes: 1. iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for Red Ruby Dessert (F02050018) does not report an iron value. Iron data for this traditional dessert preparation is not available in the HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dessert are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. saturated_fat_g (4.9 g/100 g) is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk as the dominant fat source; this value is not directly reported in the HPB source but is consistent with coconut milk composition data from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12117). 4. Dietary tags 'wholegrain' removed from input as water chestnut coated in tapioca flour does not qualify as a wholegrain food. 5. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca starch GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care. No direct GI measurement for red ruby dessert as a composite dish was found in approved sources.
